title: What’s been happening with NEM-wide demand so far this summer
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s been happening with NEM-wide demand so far this summer" posted at 24-12-2015 01:33:00 PM is: The article reflects a review of the electricity demand trends in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the early part of summer 2015-16. Despite less attention due to holidays, a notable rise in demand was observed, peaking above 30,000 MW on December 18th, surpassing the highest demand recorded in the previous summer of 2014-15. The remainder of the summer period will be monitored closely to analyze trends and determine the winner of a demand forecasting competition. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Residential satisfaction/interest. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 2015 through 31st March 2016. Its root_cause is: Increased electricity demand during the early summer period. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: December 18, 2015 (also referring to past data from November 19th). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Seasonal demand fluctuation. Its causes are: Higher electricity demand observed in summer 2015-16 compared to previous summer season.

title: What impact are Queensland LNG exports having on Queensland electricity markets?
author: Graeme Bethune
classified_content: The summary of the news "What impact are Queensland LNG exports having on Queensland electricity markets?" posted at 18-12-2015 04:06:00 PM is: The news discusses the impact of Queensland's LNG exports on electricity markets, highlighting that LNG projects, including QCLNG, GLNG, and APLNG, are operational and undergoing production ramp-ups. These projects affect electricity markets primarily by increasing demand due to electrified gas processing and influencing fuel competition, with gas possibly becoming more competitive for power production. Although LNG production increased power demand slightly, it remains close to 2014 levels, with significant impact potential in the future as more trains ramp up production and contractual arrangements change. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Anticipated primarily in Q1 2016, with more significant impacts expected in late 2016 and Q1 2017. Its root cause is: LNG production and export ramp-up in Queensland. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-12-2015. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Power producers and consumers in the Queensland electricity market. Its cause type is: Economic/Operational. Its causes are: Increased LNG production leading to changes in electricity demand and market dynamics; electrification of upstream gas processing; ramp-up of LNG trains.

title: South Australia hit by two price spikes
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "South Australia hit by two price spikes" posted at 16-12-2015 11:00:00 AM is: South Australia experienced significant price spikes in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on Monday, exemplifying the volatility of the market during the summer of 2015-16. The dispatch price soared to $2384.08 at 2:35pm and spiked again to $2410.23 at 3:10pm on December 14, 2015; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Market volatility during the summer period. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-12-2015 and 16-12-2015. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic/Natural. Its causes are: Price volatility due to high demand and potential fluctuation in supply within the NEM.

title: Queensland demand soars high in the second week of summer
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland demand soars high in the second week of summer" posted at 11-12-2015 04:37:00 PM is: Demand for electricity in Queensland has surged significantly early in the summer season of 2015-16, reaching 8507MW, marking the first time this summer demand exceeded 8500MW. This increase, logged at 3:05 PM, suggests a potential for a new all-time record above 9000MW as the summer progresses. Meanwhile, New South Wales saw its demand peak at 11018MW with temperatures reaching 35°C in Sydney at 3:50 PM. Such demand trends are critical for analysts engaged in demand forecasting. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: High temperatures leading to increased air conditioning use. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-12-2015. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related demand increase. Its causes are: High temperatures, increased demand for cooling.

title: Sun Metals submits rule change request to AEMC – for optional Five Minute Settlement
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sun Metals submits rule change request to AEMC – for optional Five Minute Settlement" posted at 04-12-2015 06:00:00 PM is: Sun Metals, a zinc smelter in northern Queensland, submitted a rule change request to the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) regarding optional five-minute settlements on 4th December 2015. The proposal aims to minimize implementation costs by allowing market participants to use SCADA systems to profile demand over a thirty-minute trading interval instead of replacing meters with five-minute interval meters. Generation payments would be made based on a volume-weighted basis, and participation in the five-minute settlement would be optional, which is expected to reduce costs and accommodate participants with varying capabilities and metering systems. The application references a completed NEMMCO process from February 2003. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Market efficiency improvement initiative. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-12-2015. Its affected region is: Northern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Voluntary market participant policy. Its causes are: Implementation cost reduction, optional participation in five-minute settlement, generation payment adjustment.

title: Demand heats up in New South Wales and Queensland on the first day of summer
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand heats up in New South Wales and Queensland on the first day of summer" posted at 04-12-2015 12:15:00 PM is: The first day of summer in 2015-16 saw significant increases in electricity demand in the Australian states of New South Wales and Queensland, driven by high temperatures reaching 35°C in Sydney and 30°C in Brisbane. New South Wales' demand rose to 11,527 MW with electricity prices spiking to $365.07, while Queensland exceeded 8,000 MW at the same time. This surge reflects the typical seasonal increase in energy consumption as residents and businesses turn to cooling solutions amid rising temperatures; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (during the heatwave). Its root_cause is: Increased electricity demand due to high temperatures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-12-2015. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased demand for cooling and resulting electricity price spikes.

title: Failure to satisfy the LRET – Prospects and Implications
author: Andrew George
classified_content: The summary of the news "Failure to satisfy the LRET – Prospects and Implications" posted at 27-11-2015 08:19:00 AM is: The article discusses Australia's Large-scale Renewable Energy Target (LRET), which mandates increasing renewable electricity generation until 2020 and then maintaining 33,000 GWh annually until 2030. The target for 2015 was 18,850 GWh, requiring significant new capacity predominantly from wind energy, of 5,000MW to 6,000MW by 2020. Challenges exist due to historical growth rates, financing uncertainties, and potential legislative changes. The impact is significant if targets are unmet, possibly leading to the imposition of penalties and re-evaluation of the scheme by the government. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until 2030. Root cause is: The legislative challenge in meeting renewable targets and market conditions that hinder the necessary growth in renewable energy capacity. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 27-11-2015. Affected region is: Australia. Affected key users are: Electricity retailers, large industrial users, and potentially renewable energy investors. Cause type is: Legislative and market conditions. Causes are: The high target increase in renewable generation, financial uncertainties in projects, historical growth rates, existing policy framework, and market hesitation due to uncertainties.

title: LNG meets the NEM
author: Zhi Oh
classified_content: The summary of the news "LNG meets the NEM" posted at 26-11-2015 10:08:00 AM is: The article discusses the significant impact that LNG production will have on the gas and electricity markets in eastern Australia, particularly in the Queensland region, over the next two decades. It highlights the commencement of LNG export operations by QCLNG, GLNG, and APLNG on Curtis Island in Queensland, with these projects expected to increase gas consumption and electricity demand in the area significantly. Once fully operational, LNG production is projected to account for about 70% of gas consumption and 20% of electricity output in Queensland, leading to a 20% growth in electricity consumption by 2020. This expansion involves extensive electricity usage, primarily in the upstream gas field and processing facilities. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 2015 to beyond 2020. Its root_cause is: LNG production and export operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same date. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: LNG producers and Queensland electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Structural market change. Its causes are: The development of LNG trains, increased gas consumption, and electricity demand associated with LNG production and export activities.

title: Puzzling through three rule change requests
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Puzzling through three rule change requests" posted at 26-11-2015 06:58:00 AM is: The article discusses three rule change proposals submitted to the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) that could significantly impact energy businesses, focusing on demand response mechanisms within the National Electricity Market (NEM). The proposals include establishing a "Negawatt Buy-back Mechanism," revising rules for "Bidding in Good Faith," and mandating "Demand side obligations to bid into central dispatch." These changes aim to address inefficiencies and structural challenges within the NEM, but concerns about transparency and market dynamics remain. The analysis highlights the potential complexities and overlaps among the proposals, with a focus on how energy users manage demand responsiveness and the implications for market operations. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Proposed rule changes in the Australian energy market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-11-2015. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy users and businesses in the NEM. Its cause type is: Policy and market structure changes. Its causes are: Introduction of new demand response rules, concerns over price discovery process, and potential impacts on market transparency and efficiency.

title: Price Response of Large Energy Users could Affect Peak Summer Demand
author: David Headberry
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price Response of Large Energy Users could Affect Peak Summer Demand" posted at 25-11-2015 12:18:00 PM is: The article discusses how large energy users in sectors like manufacturing respond to high electricity prices, especially during peak summer demand in the National Electricity Market (NEM), driven by increased use of cooling appliances. Unlike the residential and commercial sectors, large energy users mitigate costs through load shedding and efficiency measures, impacting demand peaks inversely. Price-driven load shedding occurs mostly on business days and is part of strategies to address financial pressures associated with energy costs, which have risen significantly in recent years. In the long term, energy efficiency improvements and closures also alter demand. The article highlights the flexibility of large users compared to smaller commercial operators in responding to energy price spikes. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and long-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity prices and market pressure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-11-2015. Its affected region is: South Australia and Queensland primarily. Its affected key users is: Large energy users, particularly in manufacturing. Its cause type is: Financial/market-driven. Its causes is: Rising wholesale electricity prices, network revenue increases, and financial pressure on large users.

title: A meteorologist’s prognosis for peak demand for summer 2015-16
author: Rob Davis
classified_content: The summary of the news "A meteorologist’s prognosis for peak demand for summer 2015-16" posted at 25-11-2015 06:16:00 AM is: The article discusses the potential peak electricity demand for the summer of 2015-16 in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, influenced by one of the strongest El Niño events on record. Meteorologist Rob Davis from MetraWeather examines the correlation between average summer temperatures and peak demand during extreme heat days, using predictive data for different Australian regions, including Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. Each region's likelihood of peak temperatures is assessed using historical temperature data and MetraWeather's seasonal forecast, providing insights for predicting electricity demand. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2015-16. Its root_cause is: Weather condition forecasts associated with the El Niño event. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-11-2015 06:16:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market areas (Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather event forecast. Its causes are: El Niño-induced above-average temperatures, regional heat predictions.

title: What factors will contribute to peak demand over summer 2015-2016?
author: Panos Priftakis
classified_content: The summary of the news "What factors will contribute to peak demand over summer 2015-2016?" posted at 24-11-2015 07:57:00 AM is: The article explores the factors contributing to peak electricity demand during the summer of 2015-2016 in Australia, emphasizing the impact of increased air conditioner usage during heatwaves driven by the El Niño event. It highlights that the proliferation of air conditioners, now present in 74% of Australian homes, has significantly raised electricity demand during hot periods, particularly in South Australia and Victoria, where consecutive days of high temperatures strain the electricity network. The rising adoption of rooftop solar systems reduces daytime demand, shifting peak demand later into the evening as the sun sets. Industrial demand declines due to large industrial facility closures, while population growth and evolving LNG projects are pertinent to demand changes. The Australian Bureau of Meteorology predicts a significant El Niño with higher maximum temperatures, likely causing high peak demand this summer, especially on hot weekdays in regions like Brisbane. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2015-2016. Its root_cause is: Increased air conditioner usage due to significant heatwave events during an El Niño period. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 24-11-2015 to the end of summer 2015-2016. Its affected region is: Australia, with prominent impacts in South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales, Queensland. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial users, particularly those with high air conditioning usage. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand increase. Its causes are: Heatwaves during El Niño, high ownership and usage of air conditioners, reduction in industrial demand from facility closures, increase in rooftop solar generation, expected population growth, and infrastructure projects affecting base consumption.

title: Hot weather pushes NEM-wide demand higher than the peak for summer 2014-15 (and we’re not even in summer yet…)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hot weather pushes NEM-wide demand higher than the peak for summer 2014-15 (and we’re not even in summer yet…)" posted at 19-11-2015 04:13:00 PM is: The news reports that the NEM-wide electricity demand climbed to almost 30,000MW due to high temperatures, surpassing the peak demand seen in the summer of 2014-15. This surge in demand is significant as we are not yet in the summer season. The increase in demand, notably in regions like NSW and QLD, is oriented towards challenging forecasters participating in the peak demand competition for the upcoming summer 2015-16, which closes on November 27, 2015. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High temperatures. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 19-11-2015. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) within the NEM. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand.

title: It wasn’t the horse race that led the large drop in load in NSW coincident with the race
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It wasn’t the horse race that led the large drop in load in NSW coincident with the race" posted at 05-11-2015 04:34:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant drop in electricity demand in New South Wales (NSW) that coincided with the Melbourne Cup race. It clarifies that despite widespread speculation on social media, the drop in demand was not caused by the race itself. The coincidental timing led to this misconception. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Residential satisfaction. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, limited to the time of the race. Its root_cause is: Misunderstanding/speculation about coincidental timing. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 05-11-2015. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Residential consumers. Its cause type is: Speculative/social media-driven. Its causes are: Large drop in electricity demand coinciding with the Melbourne Cup race, leading to incorrect assumptions about the cause.

title: If your horse did not come in today – here’s a better chance to win a BBQ (and possibly* a bottle of Grange)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Who's the best demand forecaster in the NEM?
classified_content: The summary of the news "If your horse did not come in today – here’s a better chance to win a BBQ (and possibly* a bottle of Grange)" posted at 03-11-2015 11:59:00 AM is: The article talks about a prediction competition related to the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) where participants can win a BBQ and possibly a bottle of Grange by accurately forecasting peak electricity demand for the summer of 2015-16. It involves predicting demand peaks for various regions involved in the NEM, including the possibility of precision forecasting wind farm output. This competition is open to people working in or around the NEM except employees from the organizing company, aiming to foster engagement and analysis regarding electricity market trends. The competition utilizes an extended definition of "summer" to include the entire first quarter of 2016. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 1, 2015, to March 31, 2016. Its root_cause is: Engaging stakeholders to forecast electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: November 3, 2015, for entry to open and December 1, 2015, to March 31, 2016, as the timeframe for the forecasting. Its affected region is: The entire Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) domain. Its affected key users are: Energy sector stakeholders and forecasters participating in the competition. Its cause type is: Market engagement initiative. Its causes are: A prediction competition to engage industry stakeholders in forecasting electricity demand and peak wind production for the NEM market segments in the summer period.

title: What role might Demand Response play in a (possible) future grid featuring high levels of intermittency?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What role might Demand Response play in a (possible) future grid featuring high levels of intermittency?" posted at 26-10-2015 01:40:00 PM is: The article discusses the potential role of Demand Response in a future electricity grid characterized by high levels of intermittency. It highlights three main topics: an introduction to Demand Response, a vision of a future grid with high intermittency, and the possible roles of Demand Response in such a grid. The presentation also emphasizes the speaker's long-term advocacy for Demand Response in Australia's National Electricity Market and the development of a resource to explore its various forms. While detailed exploration of Demand Response's role was limited by time, the article invites ongoing discussion on the topic. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Exploration of future grid scenarios with high intermittency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-10-2015. Its affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Large energy users. Its cause type is: Hypothetical exploration/analysis. Its causes are: Future grid intermittency and role of Demand Response.

title: Ramp rates, for “Unserved Demand” much higher in this future world
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Ramp rates, for 'Unserved Demand' much higher in this future world" posted at 05-10-2015 03:35:00 PM is: The article discusses potential challenges and possible solutions for electricity demand management in a future scenario highly dependent on intermittent renewable energy sources like wind and solar. It highlights the expected higher ramp rates and demand curve changes necessitating enhanced technologies such as storage, demand response, and more controllable generation forms to maintain grid stability and meet peak demand. The article also notes the potential accelerated retirement of the existing fleet of thermal assets due to these dynamic demands.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, with implications projected as more renewables are integrated into the grid. Its root_cause is: Transition to a high-renewable energy world with increased intermittent supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-10-2015 03:35:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Hypothetical analysis for future scenarios. Its causes are: Increased dependence on renewable energy sources, challenges in maintaining grid stability due to higher demand variability, need for investments in storage and demand response technologies, and retirement of outdated thermal generation assets.

title: Considerable spillage, in the high intermittency “10x” scenario
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Considerable spillage, in the high intermittency “10x” scenario" posted at 25-09-2015 08:18:00 AM is: The article delves into the challenges faced by the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) in handling a hypothetical scenario involving a tenfold increase in intermittent generation capacity, specifically solar and wind. This creates significant surplus energy during peak generation times, resulting in energy spillage and economic challenges for existing intermittent sources, as they're paid based on volume generated. Consequently, this paradigmatic shift would necessitate increased peaking capacity and demand response strategies while reevaluating baseload and peaking categorizations. The scenario underscores the need for policy adjustments and diverse technological advancements to simulate the intermittent nature of supply in the future electricity landscape.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future (Hypothetical scenario discussed). Its root_cause is: Hypothetical increase in renewable energy capacity leading to spillage and economic imbalance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-09-2015. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Intermittent renewable energy sources (solar and wind). Its cause type is: Hypothetical scenario planning/analysis. Its causes are: Assumed 10x increase in capacity for intermittent sources; Significant spillage due to high capacity during peak times; Economic implications due to unchanged RET payment structure.

title: What would “demand” look like, in a “10x” intermittent scenario
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What would “demand” look like, in a “10x” intermittent scenario," posted at 18-09-2015 11:11:00 AM is: The article discusses potential scenarios for the future of the National Electricity Market (NEM) with a significantly higher percentage of energy supplied by intermittent generation sources, such as wind and solar. The focus is on understanding the role of Demand Response in this future scenario. Key considerations include assumptions about demand and supply, with the hypothesis that Scheduled Demand will continue to decline as more electricity is sourced behind the meter. The analysis involves examining historical data from the 2014-15 financial year and creating scenarios to understand the impact of increased intermittent supply on aspects such as energy spillage and peak demand challenges. Future energy supply scenarios without new technology forms the basis of this exploration. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Increased penetration of intermittent generation sources in the future energy mix. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 18-09-2015 11:11:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Hypothetical/planning scenario. Its causes is: Increased role of intermittent generation, demand response, energy spillage, peak demand challenges.

title: Spring 2015 in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spring 2015 in the NEM" posted at 11-09-2015 11:22:00 AM is: A collection of articles discussing various events and developments related to the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the spring of 2015; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). title: Yin and Yang of Wind in the South Australian region of the National Electricity Market
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yin and Yang of Wind in the South Australian region of the National Electricity Market" posted at 11-09-2015 11:09:00 AM is: The article discusses wind farm production in South Australia and its impact on the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting the complexities of electricity supply and illustrating the effects of intermittent renewable energy, such as the Merit Order Effect, on electricity prices. Using NEM-Watch, the article provides a broad overview of market conditions and the challenges of relying on simplistic views of spot price suppression. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown; it discusses ongoing themes without specifying precise future impacts. Its root_cause is: Unknown; the article provides an analysis rather than specific causality events. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-09-2015. Its affected region is: South Australian region of the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Unknown; the article targets a broad audience including new market entrants and industry stakeholders. Its cause type is: Analysis of market complexities and informative review. Its causes are: Intermittent supply from wind farms, merit order effect, and complexities of electricity market systems.

title: A further look at the idiosyncrasies of wind in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A further look at the idiosyncrasies of wind in South Australia" posted at 31-08-2015 09:29:00 AM is: The article reflects on the performance discrepancies of wind farms in South Australia versus other regions, focusing on capacity factors and resulting revenue implications due to locational factors. While some wind farms achieve high capacity factors (up to 45% in specific years), others still hover around 30%. Wind farms in South Australia face a spot revenue "penalty" due to the correlation of wind output and its impact on spot prices. This disparity is attributed to high wind farm output coinciding with lower regional demand, which depresses spot prices and affects profitability, particularly in comparison with larger regions where volume-weighted average prices typically exceed time-weighted averages. The article highlights the complexities and uncertainties of balancing wind energy production with market demands and the implications for future energy policy. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Less Relevant. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Market dynamics related to wind farm operations and pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-08-2015. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Wind farm operators and electricity market stakeholders in South Australia. Its cause type is: Economic and operational. Its causes are: Variance in wind farm capacity factors, high correlation of wind output impacting spot prices, and market structure factors.

title: Pitfalls of Demand Management for the Domestic Electricity Consumer
author: Mervyn Davies
classified_content: The summary of the news "Pitfalls of Demand Management for the Domestic Electricity Consumer" posted at 17-08-2015 09:34:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges associated with home energy management systems intended to balance electricity demand by consumers. While these systems aim to reduce network capacity requirements, they may have limited effectiveness due to the broader collective demand patterns across groups of consumers. The article scrutinizes different tariff options for demand management, including Time of Use Demand, Time of Use Energy, and Controlled Load Tariffs, and concludes that each has its potential drawbacks and benefits concerning network economics and consumer convenience. The focus is on understanding that purely smoothing individual load profiles might not achieve the desired network efficiency unless it involves substantial load shifting from peak periods. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, contingent on policy adoption. Its root cause is: Inefficiencies in demand management systems and tariff structures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-08-2015. Its affected region is: Unknown, broadly applicable to electricity markets involved in demand management practices. Its affected key users are: Domestic electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Structural with regard to energy tariff and management systems. Its causes are: The deployment of insufficiently effective home energy management systems, inappropriate tariff incentives, and the inherent complexities of customer load aggregation.

title: Seeking to understand the great Network Debate
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Seeking to understand the great Network Debate" posted at 12-08-2015 12:58:00 PM is: The article delves into the complexities and dynamics of the network costs within the National Electricity Market (NEM), focusing on the underlying boom/bust cycle that influences investment and returns. It highlights the increasing share of network costs in electricity pricing, the impacts of regulatory decisions on capital investments, and the challenges in maintaining a balance between growth and efficiency. The piece also discusses the implications of declining demand on existing infrastructure and the pressures on determining fair rates of return for network companies. Moreover, the article touches upon the evolving debate over cost-reflective pricing and how these changes affect stakeholders in the energy sector; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing. Its root_cause is: Growth in network costs and regulatory changes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-08-2015 12:58:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Major energy users and consumers. Its cause type is: Economic and regulatory dynamics. Its causes are: Increased network costs, regulatory capital investment decisions, declining demand, policy changes in cost-reflective pricing.

title: Why large energy users are concerned about last week’s machinations in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Why large energy users are concerned about last week’s machinations in South Australia" posted at 03-08-2015 02:05:00 PM is: The article discusses the concerns of large industrial energy users in South Australia regarding the volatility in spot electricity prices, largely driven by fluctuating wind generation. As more wind farms have been deployed, South Australia's reliance on wind power has increased, leading to significant spot price variations between windy and calm periods. This situation is challenging for energy users, particularly those with spot market exposure, as they face high prices when the wind is not blowing and potential negative prices when it is. Additionally, high gas prices further complicate the market dynamics. The article reflects on these concerns in the context of increasing renewable energy adoption and the decline of thermal generation, raising questions about future market stability and energy generation strategies.

Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Short-term volatility immediate and longer-term market adjustments expected. Its root_cause is: Fluctuating wind generation and high gas prices impacting spot electricity prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28th July 2015; other relevant activities continued until the end of the week. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Large industrial energy users with spot price exposure. Its cause type is: Market and generation dynamics. Its causes are: High wind generation leading to price volatility; high gas prices; broader concerns about transitioning energy market dynamics.

title: Waxing and Waning of Wind this Week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Waxing and Waning of Wind this Week" posted at 28-07-2015 01:28:00 PM is: The article discusses the fluctuation in wind energy production within the National Electricity Market (NEM) during a specific week, highlighting an all-time record peak of 3,378 MW on July 25th. The article also notes the impact of high gas prices ($10/GJ) and low wind production on electricity prices in South Australia, with prices averaging $250/MWh. The situation is compounded by the unavailability of the Pelican Point gas turbine. There are concerns among large energy users about the viability of their businesses due to the increasing use of renewables and the potential closure of traditional power stations. Additionally, the piece touches on the broader context of energy policy, mentioning the influence of renewables and a possible policy shift towards a 50% renewable target by 2030. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root cause is: Fluctuating wind production and high gas prices. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: Main date of impact July 25th, 2015, continuing to July 28th, 2015 for high electricity prices. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Large energy users. Its cause type is: Market and production factors. Its causes are: Record high wind production, low wind production, high gas prices, unavailability of Pelican Point gas turbine.

title: Know of a talented young electricity-sector engineer (a future international Cornwall scholar)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Know of a talented young electricity-sector engineer (a future international Cornwall scholar)?" posted at 30-06-2015 12:41:00 PM is: The article discusses the E.S. Cornwall Memorial Scholarship, which provides international experience opportunities for young engineers in the electricity sector. The scholarship aims to benefit both the engineers and the Australian electricity supply industry through the skills and insights gained from abroad. title: The RET Joyride
author: miles.prosser
classified_content: The summary of the news "The RET Joyride" posted at 24-06-2015 07:42:00 AM is: The article examines the Renewable Energy Target (RET) policy framework in Australia since its inception, highlighting ongoing challenges and negotiations. Despite intentions to increase renewable energy generation, the policy encountered multiple reviews, a disconnect between costs and technology, and difficulties in execution. The Warburton Review was a key milestone, revealing the oversupply of electricity and unexpected downward pressure on prices. However, negotiations concerning targets for large-scale renewable energy were complicated, with eventual agreement on a 33,000 GWh target by 2020. The discussion extends to the impact on industries like aluminium smelting, which faced significant costs, and political dynamics influenced policy decisions. The RET's future remains uncertain, with ongoing debate and potential revisions. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. The root_cause is: Frequent policy reviews and changing targets, creating instability in the legislative framework. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-06-2015 07:42:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Major electricity users, renewables investors, especially the aluminium industry. Its cause type is: Policy and legislative decision-making. Its causes are: Renewable Energy Target policy inconsistencies, changing targets, political negotiations, and lobbying by industry stakeholders.

title: Northern Power Station becomes the next domino to fall
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Northern Power Station becomes the next domino to fall" posted at 11-06-2015 01:55:00 PM is: The Northern Power Station in South Australia is facing closure, potentially as early as April next year, with a definitive closure by March 2018. This decision follows a recent fire outage at the station, which coincided with increased energy market volatility and concerns about renewable energy targets. The power station has been experiencing lower production volumes despite stable revenue per MWh, yet faces unsustainable maintenance costs amidst an overall decline in demand and the ongoing energy policy shifts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term and potentially medium-term depending on market reactions and policy changes. Its root_cause is: Unspecified maintenance costs and declining demand trends, compounded by the recent fire outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-06-2015, with closure slated for as early as April 2016 and no later than March 2018. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Internal operations. Its causes are: Coal stockpile fire, maintenance cost issues, and lower production volumes.

title: Even the liquid-fuelled plant get a run in SA this evening with Northern offline, demand higher, and wind waning
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Even the liquid-fuelled plant get a run in SA this evening with Northern offline, demand higher, and wind waning" posted at 09-06-2015 09:05:00 PM is: The fire and subsequent outage at the Northern power station in South Australia have caused an increase in electricity demand, which, amid reduced wind power generation, led to a reliance on gas and liquid-fueled generation, resulting in a spike in electricity prices to $589.50/MWh. The higher demand during the evening was a result of various factors including reduced behind-the-meter wind operations and potentially weather conditions and a preceding public holiday. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on the evening of the event. Its root_cause is: Outage of the Northern power station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-06-2015. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Equipment failure and high demand. Its causes are: Northern power station fire, reduced wind power output, high demand due to lower behind-the-meter wind operation, and factors such as weather and recent public holiday.

title: The Nyngan Solar Plant reaches full output
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Nyngan Solar Plant reaches full output" posted at 09-06-2015 12:18:00 PM is: The Nyngan Solar Plant, jointly developed by First Solar and AGL Energy, has reached its full operational capacity of 102MW. The plant's output increased in two significant steps on Saturday, 6th June, from 75MW at the start of the day to its full capacity later on. Concurrently, there was a price blip observed in South Australia, possibly related to ongoing issues at Alinta’s Northern brown-coal plant. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Increase in solar plant output and instability issues at a coal plant. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-06-2015; 06-06-2015. Its affected region is: South Australia, influenced by the market reactions. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational changes and instability. Its causes are: Increase in solar output and problems at a brown-coal plant.

title: Is the real issue related to 5 minute dispatch and 30 minute settlement?
author: greg.denton
classified_content: The summary of the news "Is the real issue related to 5 minute dispatch and 30 minute settlement?" posted at 09-06-2015 07:50:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) is considering a rule change in the National Electricity Market (NEM) to address concerns about "good faith bidding." This change focuses on the timing and reliability of market participants' bids, which have reportedly led to price spikes, especially with late bidding practices. The rule change aims to prevent manipulation of wholesale prices and ensure market fairness. However, the AEMC's analysis suggests that late bidding contributes positively to price discovery and volatility management. The real issue highlighted is the impact of averaging 5-minute dispatch prices into 30-minute settlement periods, which distorts effective bidding responses and competitive demand-side actions. It's proposed that addressing these underlying market distortions could enhance competition, rather than imposing more regulatory burdens.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Medium-term (ongoing debate with potential long-term implications). Its root cause is: Market design issues related to bid timing and settlement periods. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-06-2015. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Policy and regulatory debate. Its causes are: Bid timing irregularities, price settlement averaging issues, concerns about wholesale pricing manipulations.

title: Recapping Australia’s SolarCoaster Ride
author: Nigel Morris
classified_content: The summary of the news "Recapping Australia’s SolarCoaster Ride" posted at 28-05-2015 08:38:00 AM is: The article reflects on the transformative journey of the solar industry in Australia over the past two decades. It underscores significant milestones, from the high initial costs of solar systems to their widespread adoption and drastic cost reduction. The narrative highlights the industry’s boom in 2011 due to favorable economic conditions and government incentives, acknowledging both the challenges and successes faced by different market players. The story portrays solar energy’s ascent as a crucial component of the energy market, engaging millions of Australians and businesses, and the evolution towards more distributed energy systems. It also touches on the contentious landscape surrounding the Renewable Energy Target (RET) and emphasizes the emotional investment and enthusiasm of solar energy users. Nigel Morris, an esteemed solar industry expert, advocates for continued support and adaptability within the sector.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term evolution with no immediate impacts. Its root_cause is: Evolution of solar industry and market dynamics over 20 years. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-05-2015. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Consumers and businesses involved in solar energy usage and policy stakeholders. Its cause type is: Market and policy-driven evolution. Its causes are: High initial costs, policy incentives, market growth, price reductions, regulatory changes, consumer adoption and engagement.

title: Wind in South Australia contributes to LOR2 warnings
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind in South Australia contributes to LOR2 warnings" posted at 11-05-2015 08:51:00 AM is: The article discusses the issuance of LOR2 (Lack of Reserve) warnings by AEMO for the South Australian region, primarily due to high wind production and ongoing transmission maintenance work. This situation is described as a "credible contingency," indicating the grid is operating in a less secure state than usual. The wind conditions make it uneconomical for thermal generation to be initiated. While such events provide arguments for enhancing interconnection capacities and demand response, they also underline the potential for energy innovations like battery storage. Despite these conditions, the market's response could mitigate possible supply issues. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term, depending on weather and maintenance schedules. Its root_cause is: High wind production combined with transmission maintenance work. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-05-2015. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural and operational factors. Its causes are: High wind generation, transmission maintenance.

title: Aggregate wind farm output tops 3,200MW in the middle of the night
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aggregate wind farm output tops 3,200MW in the middle of the night" posted at 10-05-2015 02:38:00 PM is: The article discusses a recent record in wind farm output in the National Electricity Market (NEM), which reached 3,215MW during the late-night dispatch interval, surpassing previous highs. This high output represents about 16% of the scheduled demand, although the actual percentage might be lower due to non-scheduled wind output and off-peak hot water load in South Australia. This increase is attributed to new wind projects added in late 2014, contributing to low scheduled demand levels and leading to low, even negative, electricity prices in certain areas. The resolution of the Renewable Energy Target (RET) policy is expected to further influence such outcomes by 2020. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term, potentially extending to 2020. Its root_cause is: The addition of new wind projects to the grid. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 10-05-2015. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) region, particularly South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Infrastructure expansion and policy context. Its causes are: High wind output due to new projects and fluctuations in political stance on energy policy.

title: What are, and will be, business niches for aggregators?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What are, and will be, business niches for aggregators?" posted at 04-05-2015 05:29:00 AM is: The article discusses the evolving role of aggregators in the electricity supply industry, particularly within Australia's National Electricity Market. Aggregators, who play a central role in consolidating and managing various aspects of electricity delivery, payment, customer management, data, and emerging technologies like electric vehicles and battery storage, are seen as key players in adapting to the electricity sector's rapid changes. The discussion highlights the potential for aggregators to enhance network optimization, especially with the integration of mobile battery storage and distributed generation. Moreover, aggregators have diverse roles, ranging from energy retailers and brokers to data managers and demand response coordinators, thus emphasizing their significant influence on the market as the industry shifts towards more consumer-centric and technology-driven models.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The increasing complexity and diversification of the electricity supply industry. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-05-2015. Its affected region is: Primarily Australia, focusing on the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Energy companies, consumers, and industry stakeholders. Its cause type is: Systemic changes within the industry. Its causes are (a summarized list of cause events): Evolution of electricity market structures, integration of new technologies, regulatory adjustments, and consumer behavior shifts regarding energy usage and resource management.

title: Metered Generation data feed for Royalla Solar commences (6 months after plant opening)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Metered Generation data feed for Royalla Solar commences (6 months after plant opening)" posted at 20-04-2015 01:43:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has started publishing metered generation output data for the Royalla solar project, six months after its opening, alongside the Nyngan project. These are currently the only two large-scale solar plants in New South Wales whose outputs are reported by AEMO. Recent data showed a peak output at Royalla of 15.5MW, under the 30MW threshold for mandatory registration. Observations noted that fluctuations in electricity prices seemed to coincide with variation in output from these solar installations. This data greatly contributes to the understanding of solar contributions within the National Electricity Market (NEM), indicating the increasing role of solar energy. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing. Its root_cause is: Publication of metered data for large-scale solar. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-04-2015. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar energy analysts and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Data publication and observation. title: Winning the final consolation prize (Comp #2) for peak Queensland demand
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Who's the best demand forecaster in the NEM?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Winning the final consolation prize (Comp #2) for peak Queensland demand" posted at 17-04-2015 11:39:00 AM is: The article discusses the conclusion of a series of forecasting competitions focused on peak electricity demand across various regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM) for the summer of 2014-15. Peter Sherman from InterGen won the final consolation prize for predicting Queensland's peak demand most accurately, with actual peak demand reaching 8,892MW, near an all-time record. Other competitions analyzed peak demands and wind production across the NEM, with winners announced for each category. Many forecasts did not anticipate the demand levels due to lower-than-expected peaks. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. title: Queensland’s peak demand very close to a new record – more possible next summer?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland’s peak demand very close to a new record – more possible next summer?" posted at 17-04-2015 10:54:00 AM is: The article analyzes Queensland's electricity demand patterns during the summer of 2014-15, highlighting the notable peak demand of 8,892 MW on March 5, 2015, which approached the all-time record. The high demand was partially mitigated by small-scale solar PV injections. It further describes the surprising nature of this demand to market participants and outlines potential future scenarios for the following summer, stressing the possible impact of changes in gas generation and further mergers. The piece discusses the contributing factors to demand fluctuations, such as upstream CSG electricity use and potential gas supply constraints, with an eye on strategic shifts in energy generation and policy.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. title: Wind Peak Production climbs – linearly over 5 successive summers
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind Peak Production climbs – linearly over 5 successive summers" posted at 16-04-2015 06:45:00 AM is: The article discusses the observed trends in peak wind production in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the summer of 2014-15. It highlights that wind output reached a peak of 2,848MW on December 16, 2014, coinciding with an evening demand peak. Despite expectations of higher peaks due to new wind farms, this was the highest recorded. A competition to predict peak wind output showed that most participants predicted close to the actual peak, suggesting growing predictability in wind output trends. The article also notes a general increase in average wind production over the past summers, although fluctuations and a lower-than-expected peak output this season suggest wind variability. title: A review of temperatures for summer 2014-15
author: Rob Davis
classified_content: The summary of the news "A review of temperatures for summer 2014-15" posted at 15-04-2015 01:44:00 PM is: The review of Summer 2014-15 across the National Electricity Market (NEM) indicated that extreme temperatures were mostly average, with Brisbane experiencing more than its usual share of extreme heat days, contrasting with Melbourne's cooler-than-average conditions. Predictions of a strong El Nino event were not fully realized, yet Brisbane, unlike Melbourne, encountered frequent hot spells due to easterly heat distribution. Sydney had a middling summer without significant temperature extremes, showing a notable early period of warmth lacking humidity. Factors such as La Nina events remain crucial in forecasting summer temperatures, as they have historically influenced patterns in Brisbane and Sydney. As expected, Melbourne remained cooler, accurately aligning with forecasts suggesting the southern NEM would experience less intense heat, while stronger westerly winds shifted heat primarily eastward. Its relevance level is: Level 3; Its category is: Energy policy; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2014-15; Its root_cause is: Weather patterns and climatic phenomena (El Nino/La Nina); Its accident scale is: Not applicable; Its dates are: 15-04-2015; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, specifically Brisbane, Sydney, Melbourne, Adelaide; Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in the NEM region; Its cause type is: Climatic factors; Its causes are: Variations in extreme heat days influenced by climatic patterns, notably El Nino and La Nina events.

Struve, who is an energy-sector stakeholder in the South Australian Department of State Development, was only 3MW off the actual peak demand of 1,389MW recorded late in the survey period. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tassie’s “summer” demand peak comes with cold weather?" posted at 14-04-2015 06:42:00 AM is: The news article discusses the unexpected peak demand in Tasmania during the extended summer of 2014-15, which occurred on March 26th at 6:50 AM. This peak, driven by early-morning heating needs due to cold weather, reached 1,389 MW. System technical issues also led to significant demand trips, including major industrial loads. The peak demand in Tasmania was found to be highly predictable, as reflected in the accurate predictions of competition entrants. Jeremy Machet, an energy-focused student, won Competition #3 by closely predicting the peak demand, being only 5MW above the actual figure. title: Similarly, the NSW summer demand peak failed to arrive
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Similarly, the NSW summer demand peak failed to arrive," posted at 13-04-2015 08:10:00 AM is: The article analyzes the lower-than-expected peak electricity demand in New South Wales (NSW) during the summer of 2014-15. The NSW summer demand peak reached only 11,395 MW on January 23rd, which was one day after the peak in Victoria, with only a few days exceeding 11,000 MW. This underwhelming demand was consistent with expectations, as factors such as the closure of the Kurri Kurri aluminium smelter and industrial demand reduction contributed to lower electricity consumption. Despite competition entries predicting higher peaks, actual demand was at the low end of expectations, illustrating a level of uncertainty and a plausible diminished demand due to economic and weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Residential satisfaction. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: November 2014 to February 2015. Its root_cause is: Lower-than-expected peak demand due to a mild summer, economic conditions, and industrial closures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23 January 2015; 28 January 2015. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and industrial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Market condition and economic factors.
